title: Concentration & the Supply Chain
subtitle: When everyone leans on the same pillar

hook.metaphor: Imagine an entire town where every house, shop, and hospital is wired to a single power pole. Each building looks independent — but pull down that one pole and the whole town goes dark at once. Modern business has built exactly this: thousands of 'independent' firms all quietly leaning on the same few digital pillars.
hook.scenario: A single cloud provider has a global outage for a few hours. Suddenly airlines can't check in passengers, banks' apps die, shops can't take payments, and hospitals lose systems — all at the same moment. None of them share a logo. How did one outage hit everyone?

learn: **Concentration risk** is the danger of too many critical things depending on the *same* single point. At the level of one firm, it's relying on one vendor for something vital. At the level of the *whole system*, it's thousands of firms all depending on the same handful of giant providers — the town wired to one pole.

This is the dark side of efficiency. Everyone uses the same few cloud platforms, payment networks, and core software because they're excellent and cheap — but that very concentration means a single failure cascades across the entire economy at once (our simultaneous outage).

**Systemic risk** is the grown-up name for this: when one node is so widely depended-upon that its failure threatens the stability of the whole system. Regulators now worry about specific cloud and tech providers becoming 'too critical to fail' — which is why regimes like the EU's **DORA** create oversight of **critical third-party providers** directly.

The supply chain also runs *deep*: a **supply-chain attack** compromises one widely-used software vendor to reach all its customers at once (the cyber link again). One poisoned pillar, thousands of victims.

Defences are hard but real: **diversification** (don't put everything with one provider — though it costs more), **multi-region / multi-cloud** designs, mapping your critical dependencies so you actually *know* where the pillars are, and resilience planning for 'what if our biggest provider vanishes for a day?'
bridge: So at work, you can look past the comforting illusion of independence and ask: *what single pillar, if it fell, would take down many of our critical services at once — and does the rest of our industry lean on it too?* The cheapest, most efficient setup (everyone on one provider) is often the most concentrated — and concentration is invisible right up until the lights go out.

interactions.title: One pole, whole town dark
interactions.prompt: A single cloud outage simultaneously hit airlines, banks, and shops. The deeper lesson?

options.label: Bad luck — unrelated companies all had a bad day.
options.feedback: It wasn't unrelated or unlucky. They shared one hidden pillar — the same cloud provider. That's concentration/systemic risk, and it's structural, not random.

options.label: Concentration risk — many 'independent' firms leaned on the same provider.
options.feedback: Exactly. The efficiency of everyone using the same few giants creates a shared single point of failure that cascades system-wide when it breaks.

options.label: Proof that cloud computing should never be used.
options.feedback: Too far — cloud brings huge benefits. The lesson isn't 'avoid cloud', it's 'understand and manage your concentration' via diversification, mapping, and resilience.

interactions.title: Supply-chain vocabulary
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Too many critical things depending on one point
One node's failure threatening the whole system
A vendor so widely relied on regulators oversee it
Compromising one vendor to reach all its customers
Spreading reliance so no single failure is fatal

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Concentration risk is a whole town wired to one power pole — when everyone leans on the same few cloud and tech giants, a single failure goes system-wide, so you map your pillars and diversify the critical ones.”

checkpoint.q: Concentration risk is the danger that…
options: You have too many different vendors
Too many critical things depend on the same single point of failure
checkpoint.explain: Whether one firm on one vendor, or a whole industry on one provider — shared single points of failure cascade.

checkpoint.q: Why do regulators (e.g. via DORA) now oversee 'critical third-party providers' directly?
options: Some providers are so widely depended-on that their failure is a systemic threat
checkpoint.explain: When a single provider becomes 'too critical to fail', its risk affects the whole financial system.

checkpoint.q: A practical defence against concentration risk is…
options: Putting everything with one provider for simplicity
Diversification and mapping your critical dependencies
Using more of the same single pillar
checkpoint.explain: Spread critical reliance, know where your pillars are, and plan for a major provider vanishing.
